title: Index and key pages
subtitle: Setting up an index, legend, and key at the front of a journal or notebook
excerpt: An index lets you retrieve content from a journal that spans weeks or months. A key explains your shorthand symbols and colour codes. Neither requires a pre-printed journal: both can be set up from scratch in any blank notebook using a few pages at the front. This tutorial covers two index formats and a simple symbol key layout.
sourceNotes: Index and key pages for journals draw on analogue information management traditions documented in commonplace book instructions and modern bullet journal practice. The techniques described are a synthesis of well-established notebook organisation methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A page at the front of a journal where topic names and their page numbers are recorded, allowing any entry to be found quickly without reading through the whole journal.
A legend page that defines the symbols, marks, and colour codes used throughout the journal. A key prevents the need to re-decipher personal shorthand over time.
An index populated over time as entries are added, rather than compiled retrospectively. Each time a new topic appears, it is added to the index with its page number. No retrospective search needed.

content.text: An
index page
is useful in any journal longer than a month: it turns a pile of dated pages into something retrievable. A
key

page pays off when you use consistent symbols across many pages, such as ticks for done, circles for waiting, stars for priority. Both are set up once at the front of the notebook and maintained as you go. The most practical approach is the
running index
: you add entries to it as they happen, so the index is never out of date.
What you need

attrs.heading: Kit for index and key pages

items.name: Metal ruler (for ruling columns and section lines)
Washi tape in 2 to 3 widths (optional, for colour-coded section separators)

content.text: Setting up the index page
Two-column alphabetical index
Reserve the first two pages of the notebook as index pages. Rule them into two columns with a vertical line down the centre of each page spread.

Divide each column into 13 sections (26 sections across the four columns), one per letter of the alphabet. Label each section with a letter. The sections need not be equal height: estimate space by anticipated use (frequent letters get more space).
When a topic appears in the journal for the first time, add it to the index under its first letter, with the page number. Add subsequent page references after each entry as they occur.
Topic-first running index

Instead of alphabetical columns, list entries in the order they first appear. Two columns on the page: left column for the topic name, right column for page numbers. No alphabetical sorting needed. Add entries at the bottom of the list as they appear. This format works well if the journal covers a small number of recurring topics.
Setting up the key page
Reserve one page (on the inside front cover or the page immediately after the index) for the key.

Draw each symbol you use in a column on the left, then write its meaning beside it. Symbols for tasks (bullet, open circle, dash), events (triangle, flag), and notes (plain bullet) are the standard set. Add any personal shorthand, colour codes, or washi tape meanings.
Add to the key whenever you introduce a new symbol. A key only works if it is kept current.
